news_summary: Scientists and researchers have made remarkable advances by transforming methane into medicine, uncovering insights about Earth’s earliest animals, progressing aeronautics through experimental flight, and developing brain cell chips capable of learning complex tasks.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques et les chercheurs ont réalisé des avancées remarquables en transformant le méthane en médicament, en découvrant des informations sur les premiers animaux de la Terre, en faisant progresser l'aéronautique grâce à des vols expérimentaux et en développant des puces neuronales capables d'apprendre des tâches complexes.
news_summary_es: Los científicos e investigadores han logrado avances notables al transformar el metano en medicina, descubrir información sobre los primeros animales de la Tierra, avanzar en la aeronáutica mediante vuelos experimentales y desarrollar chips de células cerebrales capaces de aprender tareas complejas.

articles.title: Scientists turn methane into medicine in stunning breakthrough
articles.summary: Scientists have unveiled a breakthrough way to turn natural gas—long burned as fuel—into valuable chemical building blocks for medicines and other high-demand products. By designing a clever iron-based catalyst powered by LED light, researchers managed to activate stubborn molecules like methane and transform them into complex compounds, even creating the hormone therapy drug dimestrol directly from methane for the first time.
articles.content:
Natural gas is one of the most plentiful energy resources on Earth. It is made mostly of methane, along with ethane and propane. Today, it is primarily burned for heat and electricity, a process that releases greenhouse gases. For years, researchers and industry leaders have tried to find ways to convert these simple hydrocarbons directly into useful chemicals instead of burning them. The challenge is that methane and similar gases are extremely stable and do not react easily, which has limited their use as sustainable raw materials for manufacturing.

A research team led by Martín Fañanás at the Centre for Research in Biological Chemistry and Molecular Materials (CiQUS) at the University of Santiago de Compostela has now developed a new method to transform methane and other components of natural gas into versatile chemical "building blocks" that can be used to make high value products, including pharmaceuticals. The study, published in Science Advances, marks an important step toward a more sustainable and circular chemical economy.

In a landmark demonstration, the CiQUS team synthesized a bioactive compound directly from methane for the first time. The compound, dimestrol, is a non-steroidal estrogen used in hormone therapy. Producing such a complex molecule from methane highlights the potential of this approach to turn an abundant, inexpensive gas into sophisticated and commercially important chemicals.

Methane Activation and Selective Allylation

The researchers focused on a reaction known as allylation. This process attaches a small chemical fragment called an allyl group to a gas molecule, effectively giving it a functional "handle" (an allyl group) that chemists can build on in later steps. With this handle in place, the modified molecule can be transformed into a wide range of products, from pharmaceutical ingredients to common industrial chemicals.

One major obstacle was the tendency of the catalytic system to trigger unwanted chlorination reactions, which created byproducts and reduced efficiency. Controlling these side reactions was essential to making the process practical.

Custom Iron Catalyst Controls Free Radicals

To solve this problem, the team designed a specialized supramolecular catalyst. "The core of this breakthrough lies in designing a catalyst based on a tetrachloroferrate anion stabilized by collidinium cations, which effectively modulates the reactivity of the radical species generated in the reaction medium," explains Prof. Fañanás. "The formation of an intricate network of hydrogen bonds around the iron atom sustains the photocatalytic reactivity required to activate the alkane, while simultaneously suppressing the catalyst's tendency to undergo competing chlorination reactions. This creates an optimal environment for the selective allylation reaction to proceed."

In simpler terms, the catalyst carefully manages highly reactive radical intermediates so they drive the desired transformation without causing unwanted side reactions.

Sustainable Photocatalysis Using Iron and LED Light

Beyond its chemical precision, the method also stands out for its environmental advantages. It relies on iron, which is inexpensive, widely available, and far less toxic than the rare and precious metals often used in catalytic chemistry. The reaction runs under relatively mild temperatures and pressures and is powered by LED light. Together, these features reduce energy demands and environmental impact.

This discovery is part of a larger research effort supported by the European Research Council (ERC) aimed at upgrading the primary components of natural gas into more valuable chemicals. In related work published in Cell Reports Physical Science, the same group reported a method for directly combining these gases with acid chlorides to produce industrially important ketones in a single step. Both advances rely on photocatalysis and strengthen CiQUS's position as a leader in developing innovative strategies to use abundant raw materials more effectively.

Toward a Circular Chemical Economy

Converting natural gas into flexible chemical intermediates could expand industrial options and gradually decrease reliance on traditional petrochemical feedstocks. The research benefits from the strong scientific environment at CiQUS, which holds the CIGUS accreditation from the Galician government in recognition of its research excellence and impact. The center also receives key funding from the European Union through the Galicia FEDER 2021-2027 Program, supporting scientific progress with clear potential for technology transfer and broader socioeconomic benefits.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where researchers developed a novel, sustainable method to convert methane, a stable and abundant natural gas component, into valuable chemical building blocks including pharmaceuticals. This advancement has broad implications for reducing greenhouse gas emissions by providing alternatives to burning natural gas, promoting a circular chemical economy, and using environmentally friendly catalysts. The detailed explanation of the catalyst design, environmental benefits, and potential industrial impact demonstrates substantial substance and significance.
articles.personality_title: Scientists convert methane gas into medicine using new iron catalyst
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Methane is the main part of natural gas, a common fuel that is usually burned for energy, releasing greenhouse gases. Scientists have long wanted to find ways to turn methane directly into useful chemicals instead of burning it, but methane is very stable and hard to change.

**What happened** – A team led by Martín Fañanás at the University of Santiago de Compostela created a special iron-based catalyst powered by LED light. This catalyst helps turn methane into complex chemical compounds. For the first time, they made dimestrol, a hormone therapy drug, directly from methane. The catalyst controls chemical reactions carefully to avoid unwanted byproducts.

**Impact** – This method uses iron, which is cheap and less toxic than other metals, and runs at mild conditions using LED light, making it environmentally friendly. Turning methane into valuable chemicals instead of burning it could reduce pollution and open new ways to make medicines and industrial products. This approach supports a circular chemical economy where raw materials are used more efficiently.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue improving this catalyst and explore making other important chemicals from natural gas. This work may lead to new industrial processes that reduce waste and pollution. The project is supported by the European Research Council and could inspire more sustainable chemical manufacturing worldwide.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists developed a new iron catalyst that transforms methane gas into valuable medicines and chemicals using light, offering a cleaner alternative to burning natural gas.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques transforment le méthane en médicament grâce à un nouveau catalyseur en fer
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le méthane est la principale composante du gaz naturel, un combustible souvent brûlé pour produire de l'énergie, ce qui libère des gaz à effet de serre. Les scientifiques cherchent depuis longtemps à transformer directement le méthane en produits chimiques utiles, mais le méthane est très stable et difficile à modifier.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par Martín Fañanás à l'Université de Saint-Jacques-de-Compostelle a créé un catalyseur spécial à base de fer activé par une lumière LED. Ce catalyseur permet de transformer le méthane en composés chimiques complexes. Pour la première fois, ils ont fabriqué du dimestrol, un médicament utilisé en thérapie hormonale, directement à partir du méthane. Le catalyseur contrôle précisément les réactions chimiques pour éviter les sous-produits indésirables.

**Impact** – Cette méthode utilise du fer, un métal peu coûteux et moins toxique que d'autres, et fonctionne à des conditions douces avec une lumière LED, ce qui est plus respectueux de l'environnement. Transformer le méthane en produits chimiques utiles au lieu de le brûler pourrait réduire la pollution et ouvrir de nouvelles voies pour fabriquer des médicaments et des produits industriels. Cette approche soutient une économie chimique circulaire où les matières premières sont utilisées plus efficacement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à améliorer ce catalyseur et à explorer la fabrication d'autres produits chimiques importants à partir du gaz naturel. Ce travail pourrait conduire à de nouveaux procédés industriels réduisant les déchets et la pollution. Le projet est soutenu par le Conseil européen de la recherche et pourrait inspirer une chimie plus durable dans le monde.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont mis au point un nouveau catalyseur en fer qui transforme le méthane en médicaments et produits chimiques grâce à la lumière, offrant une alternative plus propre à la combustion du gaz naturel.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos convierten el metano en medicina gracias a un nuevo catalizador de hierro
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El metano es el componente principal del gas natural, un combustible que se quema para producir energía y que libera gases de efecto invernadero. Durante años, los científicos han intentado transformar el metano directamente en productos químicos útiles, pero el metano es muy estable y difícil de cambiar.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo liderado por Martín Fañanás en la Universidad de Santiago de Compostela creó un catalizador especial a base de hierro que funciona con luz LED. Este catalizador ayuda a convertir el metano en compuestos químicos complejos. Por primera vez, fabricaron dimestrol, un medicamento para terapia hormonal, directamente a partir del metano. El catalizador controla cuidadosamente las reacciones para evitar productos secundarios no deseados.

**Impacto** – Este método usa hierro, que es barato y menos tóxico que otros metales, y funciona a temperaturas suaves con luz LED, lo que lo hace más ecológico. Convertir el metano en productos químicos valiosos en lugar de quemarlo podría reducir la contaminación y abrir nuevas formas de fabricar medicinas y productos industriales. Este enfoque apoya una economía química circular que usa mejor las materias primas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán mejorando el catalizador y explorando cómo fabricar otros químicos importantes a partir del gas natural. Este trabajo podría llevar a procesos industriales nuevos que reduzcan residuos y contaminación. El proyecto cuenta con apoyo del Consejo Europeo de Investigación y podría impulsar una química más sostenible a nivel mundial.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos desarrollaron un nuevo catalizador de hierro que transforma el metano en medicinas y productos químicos usando luz, ofreciendo una alternativa más limpia a la quema del gas natural.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing, stylized iron catalyst molecule delicately holding a radiant methane gas sphere, with intricate, softly glowing hydrogen bond networks surrounding the iron core, all illuminated by gentle LED-like light beams, symbolizing the transformation of natural gas into valuable medicine through sustainable photocatalysis.

articles.title: MIT study finds Earth’s first animals were likely ancient sea sponges
articles.summary: Scientists at MIT have found compelling chemical evidence that Earth’s earliest animals were likely ancient sea sponges. Hidden inside rocks over 541 million years old are rare molecular “fingerprints” that match compounds made by modern demosponges. After testing rocks, living sponges, and lab-made molecules, researchers confirmed the signals came from life — not geology. The discovery suggests sponges were thriving in the oceans well before most other animal groups appeared.
articles.content:
Researchers at MIT have uncovered new chemical clues in extremely old rocks that suggest some of the earliest animals on Earth were likely ancestors of modern sea sponges.

Reporting in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, the team describes the discovery of "chemical fossils" preserved in rocks more than 541 million years old. These chemical fossils are traces of biological molecules once produced by living organisms that were later buried, altered, and locked into sediment for hundreds of millions of years.

The newly identified molecules belong to a group called steranes, which are stable remnants of sterols such as cholesterol that form part of the cell membranes of complex life. By analyzing their structure, the scientists linked these steranes to demosponges, a major group of sea sponges. Today, demosponges appear in many shapes, sizes, and colors and live throughout the world's oceans as soft filter feeders. Their ancient relatives were likely similar in being soft bodied marine organisms.

"We don't know exactly what these organisms would have looked like back then, but they absolutely would have lived in the ocean, they would have been soft-bodied, and we presume they didn't have a silica skeleton," says Roger Summons, the Schlumberger Professor of Geobiology Emeritus in MIT's Department of Earth, Atmospheric and Planetary Sciences (EAPS).

The presence of these sponge specific chemical signatures strengthens the case that ancestors of demosponges were among the first animals to evolve, emerging well before most other major animal groups.

The research team includes lead author Lubna Shawar, a former MIT EAPS Crosby Postdoctoral Fellow who is now a research scientist at Caltech, along with Summons and colleagues Gordon Love of the University of California at Riverside, Benjamin Uveges of Cornell University, Alex Zumberge of GeoMark Research in Houston, Paco Cárdenas of Uppsala University in Sweden, and José Luis Giner of the State University of New York College of Environmental Science and Forestry.

Revisiting a 2009 Discovery in Precambrian Rocks

This work builds on a study the group first published in 2009. At that time, they analyzed rocks from an outcrop in Oman and detected an unusually high concentration of steranes derived from 30-carbon (C30) sterols. These rare steroid molecules appeared to originate from ancient sea sponges.

The rocks dated to the Ediacaran Period, which lasted from about 635 million to 541 million years ago, just before the Cambrian Period when complex multicellular life rapidly diversified. The earlier findings suggested that sponges existed long before the Cambrian explosion and may have been among the planet's earliest animals.

Not everyone agreed. Some researchers proposed that the C30 steranes might have been produced by other organisms or even formed through nonbiological geological processes.

The new study adds weight to the sponge hypothesis. The team identified another distinctive chemical fossil in the same Precambrian rocks that is highly likely to have come from living organisms rather than from chemistry alone.

Rare Sterols and the Search for Early Animal Life

As in their previous investigations, the researchers examined Ediacaran age rocks collected from drill cores and outcrops in Oman, western India, and Siberia. They searched for steranes, which are stable versions of sterols found in all eukaryotes (plants, animals, and any organism with a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles).

"You're not a eukaryote if you don't have sterols or comparable membrane lipids," Summons says.

Sterols share a core structure made of four connected carbon rings. Different organisms modify that structure by adding carbon side chains and other chemical groups, depending on the genes they carry. In humans, cholesterol contains 27 carbon atoms, while plant sterols typically contain 29.

"It's very unusual to find a sterol with 30 carbons," Shawar says.

The earlier research had identified a 30-carbon sterol tied to a specific enzyme encoded by a gene common in demosponges. In the new analysis, the team realized that the same gene could also produce an even rarer 31-carbon sterol (C31). When they reexamined their rock samples, they detected abundant C31 steranes alongside the previously identified C30 forms.

"These special steranes were there all along," Shawar says. "It took asking the right questions to seek them out and to really understand their meaning and from where they come."

Laboratory Tests Confirm Biological Origin

To confirm the source, the scientists studied living demosponges and found that some species produce C31 sterols, the biological precursors of the C31 steranes preserved in rock. They then synthesized eight different C31 sterols in the laboratory to serve as reference compounds. After subjecting these molecules to conditions that mimic burial and geological transformation over millions of years, they compared the results with the ancient samples.

Only two of the eight synthesized sterols transformed into compounds that matched the C31 steranes found in the rocks. The absence of the other six products indicates that the molecules were not created by random chemical reactions in the environment.

Together, evidence from rock chemistry, modern sponges, and laboratory experiments supports the conclusion that the steranes originated from living organisms. Those organisms were most likely early ancestors of demosponges, which still retain the ability to produce similar compounds today.

"It's a combination of what's in the rock, what's in the sponge, and what you can make in a chemistry laboratory," Summons says. "You've got three supportive, mutually agreeing lines of evidence, pointing to these sponges being among the earliest animals on Earth."

"In this study we show how to authenticate a biomarker, verifying that a signal truly comes from life rather than contamination or non-biological chemistry," Shawar adds.

Expanding the Search for the First Animals

Now that C30 and C31 sterols appear to be reliable indicators of ancient sponges, the researchers plan to examine rocks from other parts of the world. So far, the samples indicate that these sponges lived during the Ediacaran Period. With additional material, the team hopes to pinpoint more precisely when some of the earliest animals first emerged.

This research was supported, in part, by the MIT Crosby Fund, the Distinguished Postdoctoral Fellowship program, the Simons Foundation Collaboration on the Origins of Life, and the NASA Exobiology Program.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery providing compelling chemical evidence that ancient sea sponges were among Earth's earliest animals. This breakthrough advances understanding of early animal evolution with broad implications for biology and earth sciences. The study is well-detailed, based on rigorous analysis of ancient rocks, modern organisms, and lab experiments, demonstrating a clear positive impact on scientific knowledge.
articles.personality_title: MIT study reveals ancient sea sponges as Earth’s first animals
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wondered which animals were the very first to appear on Earth. The Ediacaran Period, over 541 million years ago, is a key time just before many complex animals suddenly appeared. Researchers have searched for clues in ancient rocks to find traces of life from that time.

**What happened** – A team from MIT found special chemical signs called steranes inside rocks older than 541 million years. These chemicals match those made by modern sea sponges called demosponges. The researchers checked rocks from Oman, India, and Siberia, studied living sponges, and recreated molecules in the lab. This confirmed the chemicals came from real living creatures, not from non-living processes.

**Impact** – This discovery strengthens the idea that sea sponges were some of the first animals on Earth. Finding both 30-carbon and rare 31-carbon steranes shows these creatures existed before most other animals. It changes how scientists understand early animal evolution by providing strong chemical proof instead of just fossils.

**What's next step** – The research team plans to look for these sponge chemical signs in more places around the world. By studying more rocks, they hope to learn exactly when these first animals appeared and how they lived. This could help fill in missing parts of Earth’s early history.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Chemical evidence from ancient rocks shows that sea sponges were likely Earth’s earliest animals, appearing well before other major animal groups.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une étude du MIT révèle que les premières animaux sur Terre étaient des éponges marines anciennes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques se demandent depuis longtemps quels animaux sont apparus en premier sur Terre. La période édiacarienne, il y a plus de 541 millions d’années, est un moment clé juste avant l’apparition soudaine de nombreux animaux complexes. Les chercheurs cherchent des indices dans des roches anciennes pour trouver des traces de vie de cette époque.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe du MIT a trouvé des signes chimiques spéciaux appelés stéranes dans des roches âgées de plus de 541 millions d’années. Ces produits chimiques correspondent à ceux fabriqués par des éponges marines modernes appelées démosponges. Les chercheurs ont examiné des roches d’Oman, d’Inde et de Sibérie, étudié des éponges vivantes et recréé des molécules en laboratoire. Cela a confirmé que ces produits chimiques provenaient de véritables organismes vivants, pas de processus non vivants.

**Impact** – Cette découverte renforce l’idée que les éponges marines faisaient partie des premiers animaux sur Terre. La découverte de stéranes à 30 et 31 atomes de carbone montre que ces créatures existaient avant la plupart des autres animaux. Cela change la façon dont les scientifiques comprennent l’évolution des premiers animaux en fournissant une preuve chimique solide plutôt que seulement des fossiles.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe de recherche prévoit de chercher ces signes chimiques d’éponges dans d’autres régions du monde. En étudiant plus de roches, ils espèrent savoir exactement quand ces premiers animaux sont apparus et comment ils vivaient. Cela pourrait aider à compléter l’histoire ancienne de la Terre.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des preuves chimiques dans des roches anciennes montrent que les éponges marines étaient probablement les premiers animaux sur Terre, apparus bien avant les autres grands groupes animaux.
articles.personality_title_es: Estudio del MIT revela que las primeras animales de la Tierra fueron esponjas marinas antiguas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han querido saber por mucho tiempo cuáles fueron los primeros animales en aparecer en la Tierra. El Período Ediacárico, hace más de 541 millones de años, es un momento importante justo antes de que aparecieran muchos animales complejos. Los investigadores buscan pistas en rocas antiguas para encontrar rastros de vida de esa época.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo del MIT encontró señales químicas especiales llamadas esteranos dentro de rocas de más de 541 millones de años. Estas sustancias químicas coinciden con las que producen las esponjas marinas modernas llamadas demospongias. Los investigadores estudiaron rocas de Omán, India y Siberia, examinaron esponjas vivas y recrearon moléculas en el laboratorio. Esto confirmó que las sustancias venían de seres vivos reales, no de procesos no vivos.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento fortalece la idea de que las esponjas marinas fueron algunos de los primeros animales en la Tierra. Encontrar esteranos de 30 y 31 átomos de carbono muestra que estas criaturas existían antes que la mayoría de los otros animales. Cambia cómo los científicos entienden la evolución de los primeros animales al ofrecer pruebas químicas fuertes en lugar de solo fósiles.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea buscar estas señales químicas de esponjas en más lugares del mundo. Al estudiar más rocas, esperan saber exactamente cuándo aparecieron estos primeros animales y cómo vivían. Esto podría ayudar a completar la historia temprana de la Tierra.

**Resumen en una frase** – Evidencias químicas en rocas antiguas muestran que las esponjas marinas fueron probablemente los primeros animales de la Tierra, apareciendo mucho antes que otros grupos animales importantes.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate, softly glowing underwater scene from the ancient Ediacaran ocean, featuring delicate, abstract shapes inspired by soft-bodied demosponges gently filtering water, surrounded by sediment layers embedded with subtle, stylized molecular patterns symbolizing ancient chemical fossils, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones and muted blues with a painterly, detailed style.

articles.title: NASA’s Home for Experimental Flight Advances Aeronautics Mission
articles.summary: Nestled in the Mojave Desert, NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards, California, pushes the boundaries of flight to advance the agency’s aeronautics mission. This is where Chuck Yeager broke the sound barrier and engineers are now pioneering the future of high-speed, autonomous, and electrified aircraft. Armstrong contributes to NASA’s broader mission of innovation and […]
articles.content:
Nestled in the Mojave Desert, NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards, California, pushes the boundaries of flight to advance the agency’s aeronautics mission. This is where Chuck Yeager broke the sound barrier and engineers are now pioneering the future of high-speed, autonomous, and electrified aircraft. Armstrong contributes to NASA’s broader mission of innovation and collaboration, leveraging its uniquely capable location.

The story begins in 1947, when 13 engineers and technicians from NASA’s predecessor, the National Advisory Committee for Aeronautics, arrived at Muroc Army Airfield – now Edwards Air Force Base – in Southern California’s high desert to establish the Station for High-Speed Research. Their mission was to prepare for the first supersonic research flights of the X-1 rocket plane. The Bell X-1 became the first aircraft to fly faster than the speed of sound in level flight, a historic milestone that marked the dawn of a new era in aviation and helped cement Edwards Air Force Base as a cornerstone of NASA’s flight research enterprise.

Today, NASA’s mission continues that tradition, supporting cutting-edge projects in aeronautics like the X-59 quiet supersonic technology aircraft, hypersonic research, and emerging technologies in advanced air mobility, with flight testing led at NASA Armstrong in collaboration with other NASA centers and industry partners.

Why Edwards?

NASA Armstrong’s location at Edwards Air Force Base supports NASA’s flight research that would be difficult or impossible elsewhere, offering unmatched access to the largest secure flight test range in the nation equipped with specialized testing instrumentation. The base spans roughly 470 square miles of mission-critical terrain, including Rogers Dry Lake’s 44-square-mile surface. This range provides extensive restricted airspace enabling safe, complex flight-testing scenarios for NASA teams across multiple programs.

Almost from the start of aeronautical advancements, the region’s natural geography played a critical role. In 1937, nearly all the U.S. Army Air Corp’s fleet conducted maneuvers above Rogers Dry Lake – then known as Muroc Dry Lake – a vast, flat expanse formed by ancient geological processes that serves as a unique emergency landing site. Its hard-packed surface and wide-open area provide a natural safety net for experimental aircraft, offering a margin of safety that’s critical during high-risk missions.

With the U.S. involvement in World War II, the area’s importance grew, bringing additional resources, new facilities, and a focus on research, and experimentation with new aircraft designs. Today, the airspace above the region includes the Bell X-1 Supersonic Corridor, a designated section of restricted airspace within the Edwards test range. This corridor provides a safe, controlled environment for supersonic and transonic flight testing, enabling precision maneuvers at high speeds over the Mojave Desert. Combined with nearly year-round flying weather and low population density, this unique airspace supports uninterrupted flight operations for NASA’s aeronautics programs.

Used as a directional indicator the compass rose guides pilots flying test and experimental aircraft like the Pilatus PC-12 in the vast airspace over NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards, California. This Pilatus PC-12 based out of NASA’s Glenn Research Center in Cleveland is being flown for a series of familiarization flights for NASA’s Armstrong pilots and crew. These familiarization flights supported communication, navigation and surveillance evaluations for Advanced Air Mobility research. NASA/Jim Ross

A culture of innovation

NASA’s X-plane legacy is deeply rooted in its history. From the X-1 to the X-59, NASA has developed dozens of X-planes – many flight-tested at Edwards with contributions by Armstrong and other NASA centers. These experimental aircraft were designed to push the boundaries of flight and test new technologies. At Edwards, NASA teams have tested everything from lifting body designs – critical for spacecraft and reentry research – to digital fly-by-wire systems, which have become standard in commercial aviation.

This culture of innovation continues today as NASA’s aeronautics team – leveraging Armstrong’s flight research expertise – advances advanced air mobility, electrified propulsion, and autonomous flight systems. The center’s location and infrastructure enable rapid prototyping and testing, accelerating NASA’s ability to mature next generation aviation technologies.

Partnerships with the U.S. Air Force further strengthen NASA’s capabilities. Shared resources, coordinated airspace management, and joint operations allow NASA researchers to conduct complex missions with support and safety protocols, while collaborating across NASA centers and industry.

Supporting a broad mission portfolio

While Armstrong is best known for experimental aircraft, NASA’s work at Edwards supports a diverse mission portfolio. The center supports Earth science missions, airborne sensor testing, and planetary exploration. Its aircraft – including ER-2 and Gulfstream – carry instruments that study climate, weather, and atmospheric composition, contributing vital data to NASA’s science goals in partnership with agency science teams.

Edwards’ location and infrastructure enable these missions by providing access to high-altitude corridors, stable flying conditions, and the ability to integrate new technologies quickly. Whether it’s testing sensors for Mars exploration or flying over hurricanes to collect data, NASA’s airborne science, supported by Armstrong’s flight operations, advance agency priorities.

Justin Hall lands the Dryden Remotely Operated Integrated Drone 2 (DROID 2) aircraft at NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards, California, on Aug. 22, 2023.

Milestones that matter

NASA’s flight research heritage at Edwards includes milestones that have shaped aviation history:

Each of these achievements reflects NASA collaboration, drawing on location, infrastructure, and culture to deliver agency impact. As aviation enters a new era of fuel savings, autonomy, and accessibility, NASA’s aeronautics team – through flight research at Armstrong and elsewhere – remains steady to test the technologies that will define the future of flight.

NASA’s X-59 quiet supersonic research aircraft cruises above Palmdale and Edwards, California, during its first flight, Tuesday, Oct. 28, 2025. The aircraft traveled to NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards, California. NASA/Lori Losey

Looking ahead

With growing interest in advanced air mobility, high-speed flight research, and new aircraft technologies, NASA’s integrated approach is more critical than ever. NASA Armstrong’s flight test discipline and safety frameworks contribute to agency-wide risk management and systems engineering, supporting NASA’s top priorities – from commercial supersonic technologies to the safety practices that underpin human spaceflight.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights NASA Armstrong Flight Research Center's significant contributions to advancing aeronautics technology, including pioneering high-speed, autonomous, and electrified aircraft. It details the center's unique capabilities, historical milestones, and ongoing innovations that have broad implications for the future of aviation and aerospace technology. The story focuses on a single, substantive topic with clear real-world impact and broad significance for society through improved flight technologies and safety.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s Armstrong Center leads breakthroughs in flight technology
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center in Edwards, California, has a long history of testing new aircraft and pushing the limits of flight. Located in the Mojave Desert, it was the site where Chuck Yeager first broke the sound barrier in 1947.

**What happened** – Today, Armstrong continues to develop advanced aircraft, including high-speed, autonomous, and electric planes. The center uses its unique location at Edwards Air Force Base, which offers a huge, secure test area and special airspace for supersonic flights. NASA works with the U.S. Air Force and industry partners to test new technologies like the X-59 quiet supersonic plane and advanced air mobility systems.

**Impact** – Armstrong’s work is important because it helps create safer, faster, and cleaner aircraft. The center’s location allows for complex and risky flight tests that would be hard to do elsewhere. Their research supports many missions, from improving commercial planes to carrying instruments that study Earth’s climate and prepare for space exploration.

**What's next step** – NASA will keep testing new flight technologies at Armstrong, focusing on electric propulsion, autonomous flying, and supersonic travel. These efforts will help develop future aircraft that use less fuel, reduce noise, and increase safety. The center’s collaborations will support NASA’s goals for both aviation and space missions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s Armstrong Flight Research Center is a key hub for testing groundbreaking aircraft technologies that shape the future of flight and space exploration.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le centre Armstrong de la NASA à la pointe des technologies aéronautiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le centre de recherche en vol Armstrong de la NASA à Edwards, en Californie, possède une longue histoire d’expérimentations aériennes, notamment le franchissement du mur du son par Chuck Yeager en 1947.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Aujourd’hui, Armstrong développe des avions avancés, rapides, autonomes et électriques. Le centre utilise son emplacement unique à la base aérienne Edwards, qui offre une vaste zone de test sécurisée et un espace aérien spécial pour les vols supersoniques. La NASA collabore avec l’armée de l’air américaine et des partenaires industriels pour tester des technologies comme l’avion supersonique silencieux X-59 et les systèmes de mobilité aérienne avancée.

**Impact** – Le travail d’Armstrong est important car il aide à créer des avions plus sûrs, plus rapides et plus propres. Son emplacement permet des essais complexes et risqués difficiles à réaliser ailleurs. Ces recherches soutiennent de nombreuses missions, de l’amélioration des avions commerciaux à l’étude du climat terrestre et à la préparation de l’exploration spatiale.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA continuera à tester de nouvelles technologies de vol à Armstrong, en se concentrant sur la propulsion électrique, le vol autonome et les voyages supersoniques. Ces efforts aideront à développer des avions futurs consommant moins de carburant, moins bruyants et plus sûrs. Les collaborations renforceront les objectifs de la NASA pour l’aviation et l’espace.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le centre de recherche en vol Armstrong de la NASA est un lieu clé pour tester des technologies aéronautiques innovantes qui façonnent l’avenir du vol et de l’exploration spatiale.
articles.personality_title_es: El centro Armstrong de la NASA impulsa avances en tecnología de vuelo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Centro de Investigación de Vuelo Armstrong de la NASA en Edwards, California, tiene una larga historia de pruebas de nuevas aeronaves, incluido el primer vuelo que rompió la barrera del sonido en 1947 por Chuck Yeager.

**Qué pasó** – Hoy, Armstrong sigue desarrollando aviones avanzados, rápidos, autónomos y eléctricos. El centro aprovecha su ubicación única en la Base Aérea Edwards, que ofrece un área de prueba segura y un espacio aéreo especial para vuelos supersónicos. NASA colabora con la Fuerza Aérea y socios industriales para probar tecnologías como el avión supersónico silencioso X-59 y sistemas avanzados de movilidad aérea.

**Impacto** – El trabajo de Armstrong es importante porque ayuda a crear aviones más seguros, rápidos y limpios. Su ubicación permite realizar pruebas complejas y riesgosas que serían difíciles en otro lugar. La investigación apoya muchas misiones, desde mejorar aviones comerciales hasta estudiar el clima terrestre y preparar la exploración espacial.

**Próximo paso** – NASA continuará probando nuevas tecnologías de vuelo en Armstrong, enfocándose en propulsión eléctrica, vuelo autónomo y viajes supersónicos. Estos esfuerzos ayudarán a desarrollar aviones futuros que usen menos combustible, hagan menos ruido y sean más seguros. Las colaboraciones apoyarán las metas de NASA para la aviación y el espacio.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El Centro de Investigación de Vuelo Armstrong de la NASA es un lugar clave para probar tecnologías aeronáuticas innovadoras que moldean el futuro del vuelo y la exploración espacial.

articles.title: Human brain cells on a chip learned to play Doom in a week
articles.summary: Neuron-powered computer chips can now be easily programmed to play a first-person shooter game, bringing biological computers a step closer to useful applications
articles.content:
Neuron-powered computer chips can now be easily programmed to play a first-person shooter game, bringing biological computers a step closer to useful applications

A screen image of Doom being played by human neurons on a chip Cortical Labs

A clump of human brain cells can play the classic computer game Doom. While its performance is not up to par with humans, experts say it brings biological computers a step closer to useful real-world applications, like controlling robot arms.

In 2021, the Australian company Cortical Labs used its neuron-powered computer chips to play Pong. The chips consisted of clumps of more than 800,000 living brain cells grown on top of microelectrode arrays that can both send and receive electrical signals. Researchers had to carefully train the chips to control the paddles on either side of the screen.

Advertisement

Now, Cortical Labs has developed an interface that makes it easier to program these chips using the popular programming language Python. An independent developer, Sean Cole, then used Python to teach the chips to play Doom, which he did in around a week.

“Unlike the Pong work that we did a few years ago, which represented years of painstaking scientific effort, this demonstration has been done in a matter of days by someone who previously had relatively little expertise working directly with biology,” says Brett Kagan of Cortical Labs. “It’s this accessibility and this flexibility that makes it truly exciting.”

The neuronal computer chip, which used about a quarter as many neurons as the Pong demonstration, played Doom better than a randomly firing player, but far below the performance of the best human players. However, it learnt much faster than traditional, silicon-based machine learning systems and should be able to improve its performance with newer learning algorithms, says Kagan.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Daily The latest on what’s new in science and why it matters each day. Sign up to newsletter

However, it’s not useful to compare the chips with human brains, he says. “Yes, it’s alive, and yes, it’s biological, but really what it is being used as is a material that can process information in very special ways that we can’t recreate in silicon.”

“Doom is vastly more complex than earlier demonstrations, and successfully interacting with it highlights real advances in how living neural systems can be controlled and trained,” says Andrew Adamatzky at the University of the West of England in Bristol, UK.

Steve Furber at the University of Manchester, UK, agrees that Doom is a significant level up from playing Pong, but he says there is still a lot we don’t understand about how these neurons are playing the game, such as how the neurons know what is expected of them or how they can “see” the screen with no eyes.

Even so, the jump in capability is exciting, says Yoshikatsu Hayashi at the University of Reading, UK, and brings us significantly closer to useful real-world applications, such as controlling a robotic arm with biological computers, a task which Hayashi and his colleagues are attempting with a similar computer made from jelly-like hydrogel. “[Playing Doom] is like a simpler version of controlling a whole arm,” says Hayashi.

“What’s exciting here is not just that a biological system can play Doom, but that it can cope with complexity, uncertainty, and real-time decision-making,” says Adamatzky. “That’s much closer to the kinds of challenges future biological or hybrid computers will need to handle.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough where living human brain cells on a chip have been programmed to play a complex video game, Doom, demonstrating advances in biological computing. This progress has broad implications for future real-world applications such as controlling robotic arms, representing a meaningful step forward in hybrid biological-silicon computing systems. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a novel achievement with potential large-scale benefits.
articles.personality_title: Human brain cells on a chip learn to play Doom in just one week
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been working on computers made from living brain cells. These special chips use human neurons, or brain cells, to process information differently from regular computers made of silicon.

**What happened** – An Australian company called Cortical Labs created a new way to program these neuron-powered chips using the Python programming language. In about a week, a developer taught the chips to play the video game Doom, a much more complex game than the simple Pong game these chips played in 2021.

**Impact** – This achievement shows that living brain cells on a chip can learn and make decisions in real time, even with complex tasks like playing Doom. Although the chips did not play as well as humans, they learned faster than traditional computer systems. This progress is important because it brings us closer to using biological computers for practical jobs, such as controlling robot arms.

**What's next step** – Researchers will work on making these neuron chips better by improving how they learn and respond. They also want to understand more about how the neurons process information without traditional senses like eyes. This research could lead to new kinds of hybrid computers that combine biology and technology for tasks that need quick and flexible thinking.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Living brain cells on a chip have learned to play a complex video game, marking a key step toward biological computers that could help control machines in the future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des cellules cérébrales humaines sur puce apprennent à jouer à Doom en une semaine
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des scientifiques développent des ordinateurs fabriqués à partir de cellules cérébrales vivantes. Ces puces spéciales utilisent des neurones humains pour traiter l'information différemment des ordinateurs traditionnels en silicium.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une entreprise australienne, Cortical Labs, a créé un nouveau moyen de programmer ces puces neuronales avec le langage Python. En environ une semaine, un développeur a appris aux puces à jouer au jeu vidéo Doom, un jeu beaucoup plus complexe que le simple Pong joué en 2021.

**Impact** – Cette réussite montre que des cellules cérébrales vivantes sur une puce peuvent apprendre et prendre des décisions en temps réel, même pour des tâches complexes comme jouer à Doom. Bien que les puces ne jouent pas aussi bien que les humains, elles apprennent plus vite que les systèmes informatiques traditionnels. Cette avancée rapproche de l’utilisation pratique des ordinateurs biologiques, comme pour contrôler des bras robotiques.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont améliorer ces puces neuronales en perfectionnant leur apprentissage et leur réactivité. Ils veulent aussi mieux comprendre comment les neurones traitent l’information sans les sens traditionnels comme la vue. Cela pourrait mener à de nouveaux ordinateurs hybrides combinant biologie et technologie pour des tâches nécessitant rapidité et souplesse.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des cellules cérébrales vivantes sur puce ont appris à jouer à un jeu vidéo complexe, marquant une étape clé vers des ordinateurs biologiques capables de contrôler des machines à l’avenir.
articles.personality_title_es: Células cerebrales humanas en un chip aprenden a jugar Doom en solo una semana
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Científicos están desarrollando computadoras hechas con células cerebrales vivas. Estos chips especiales usan neuronas humanas para procesar información de manera diferente a las computadoras tradicionales de silicio.

**Qué pasó** – Una empresa australiana llamada Cortical Labs creó una nueva forma de programar estos chips neuronales con el lenguaje Python. En aproximadamente una semana, un desarrollador enseñó a los chips a jugar el videojuego Doom, un juego mucho más complejo que el sencillo Pong que jugaron en 2021.

**Impacto** – Este logro demuestra que las células cerebrales vivas en un chip pueden aprender y tomar decisiones en tiempo real, incluso con tareas complejas como jugar Doom. Aunque los chips no juegan tan bien como los humanos, aprenden más rápido que los sistemas computacionales tradicionales. Este avance acerca el uso práctico de computadoras biológicas para trabajos como controlar brazos robóticos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores trabajarán para mejorar estos chips neuronales, perfeccionando su aprendizaje y respuesta. También quieren entender mejor cómo las neuronas procesan información sin sentidos tradicionales como la vista. Esta investigación podría conducir a nuevos tipos de computadoras híbridas que combinan biología y tecnología para tareas que requieren rapidez y flexibilidad.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las células cerebrales vivas en un chip aprendieron a jugar un videojuego complejo, marcando un paso clave hacia computadoras biológicas que podrían ayudar a controlar máquinas en el futuro.
articles.image_prompt: A warm and detailed painting of a glowing cluster of interconnected human brain neurons delicately resting atop a sleek microchip, with ethereal electric signals flowing between them, subtly illuminating a vintage computer screen displaying the pixelated game Doom, all rendered in soft, natural tones to symbolize the fusion of biology and technology learning together.
